# Chapter Two: The Taking

The room was dark in the way that rooms get dark in the Flotilla, where even the dimmest lanterns leave shadows thick enough to sleep in. Mira had learned to wake before the shadows thinned, before the other children stirred, when the world was just water and wood and the small sounds of things holding themselves together.

She lay still and listened. Three breaths in the darkness: Tam's shallow sleeping rattle, the twins breathing in tandem like a single organism split down the middle, and somewhere below her the creak of rope and the water moving against the hull of the Wrecker's Promise. The boat that wasn't a boat anymore, just a place that had once been a boat, held together by memory and spite and Greysalt's unwillingness to let anything die quietly.

Her mother had lived on a boat like this once. Before.

Mira didn't remember the before very well. There were impressions: the smell of salt and something metallic she couldn't name, the sound of humming that felt like being wrapped in a blanket, the weight of small hands holding her steady while the world rocked beneath her. The rest was just water, dark and cold and deep.

She sat up. The hammock swayed with her, the rough canvas scratching against her arms. She had a top hammock, which was good. The twins had the bottom bunks, and in the night they sometimes forgot they were no longer sharing a womb and kicked out at phantom siblings. Tam slept on a pallet near the door because Tam was twelve and had earned the right to choose, though the door was cold and the draft came in through gaps in the hull that Greysalt kept promising to seal.

Mira's fingers found the worn edge of her seabird before her eyes fully opened. The stuffed animal was grey and shapeless, more suggestion than substance, held together by her certainty that it mattered. It had been hers since before. Its name was Drift, because that was what seabirds did, and because the woman who gave it to her had said the name like it meant something.

*The taking*, the woman had said, pressing Drift into her small hands. *They always call it something prettier than that.*

Mira didn't remember the woman's face anymore. She remembered the words.

She held Drift against her chest and listened to the darkness breathe. In an hour, maybe less, the light would come grey and thin through the gaps in the walls. In a few hours more, she would be at the Gutter Market with the others, carrying crates and sweeping floors and earning the small coins that Greysalt said paid for her bed and her meals and the privilege of being alive in a city that didn't have room for children without families.

*Your mother is coming today*, Greysalt had told her. *She's going to be at the market. You'll see her.*

He'd said it like he was doing her a kindness. He'd said it like he was giving her something precious. He'd smiled, and his smile had a quality Mira had learned not to trust—the smile of a man who knew things he wasn't saying, who held cards close to his chest and let you feel their edges.

*She doesn't know you're coming*, he'd added. *It will be an accident. A surprise. Children like surprises.*

Mira had nodded. She'd practiced her surprise face in the small mirror that hung above the washbasin, the one that showed only part of her face at a time because the silver was corroded along the left edge. She'd made her eyes wide and her mouth open in a perfect O of delighted shock.

And then she'd gone back to her hammock and whispered to Drift.

*You could just stay*, she said to the stuffed bird, the words worn smooth from repetition. *You could stay and then I wouldn't have to miss you.*

Drift didn't answer. That was all right. Mira didn't expect answers. She expected something else entirely—something that lived in the space between the words, in the shape of what she couldn't say to anyone who might actually respond.

*I miss you*, she said instead, to the darkness, to the water, to whatever was listening. *Even when you're right there, I miss you. Isn't that strange?*

The darkness didn't answer.

The water kept moving.

Somewhere in the city, her mother was waking up in a room that wasn't a boat, in a life that didn't include her, and Mira was here holding a bird made of cloth and hope and the stubborn refusal to believe that some distances couldn't be crossed.

She pressed her face into Drift's faded wing and breathed in the smell of salt and dust and something that might have been tears, once, from someone who wasn't her.

The light was coming now. Grey and thin and slanted, the way light came through the Flotilla's patchwork walls, catching the dust motes that drifted through the room like tiny drowned things making their way slowly downward.

Tam stirred. The twins remained motionless, breathing in their synchronized rhythm, two halves of a whole that had never quite learned to be separate. Mira watched them with the particular attention of someone who had learned early that people could split in ways that looked like love and feel like something else entirely.

Greysalt had explained the arrangement when she first arrived. The twins were bonded—a bloodforge term, one of the few she knew—and separating them caused pain. The foster arrangement was a kindness. The twins needed each other, and here they could have each other, and wasn't that better than the alternative? The institution, the correction houses, the places where children who didn't fit went to be made to fit.

*The Covenant takes care of its own*, Greysalt had said. *Even the ones who aren't Covenant anymore.*

He'd looked at her when he said it. The last word had hung in the air, heavy with meaning she hadn't understood.

*Even the ones who aren't Covenant anymore.*

Her mother had been Covenant. Maren had explained that once, in the early days, before the visits stopped and the letters became less frequent and eventually became just the occasional note delivered by people who didn't give their names. Her mother had worked for an organization that dealt in endings. Her mother had been very good at endings. Her mother had wanted to stop.

*That's why you're here*, Maren had said, her voice strange and careful, the voice she used when she was trying not to say something. *That's why you're safe. They made a promise.*

Mira hadn't asked what happened to people who broke promises. She'd been four, and she'd already learned that some questions had answers that didn't help.

Now she was seven, and she still didn't ask, but she noticed things. She noticed that Greysalt's smiles didn't reach his eyes. She noticed that the other children who arrived in the foster arrangement sometimes left and didn't come back, and the ones who stayed had the particular stillness of people waiting for something they couldn't name. She noticed that the coins she earned at the Gutter Market never quite added up to what Greysalt said they should, and that the food appeared and disappeared with a regularity that suggested schedule rather than improvisation.

She noticed that Tam watched the door like someone expecting company she didn't want.

She noticed that the twins sometimes woke up screaming names neither of them recognized.

She noticed all of it, and she said nothing, because noticing was safer than knowing, and knowing was dangerous, and she was seven years old and tired in ways that sleep couldn't fix.

The light strengthened. The room filled with the sounds of waking: coughs and shuffles and the particular groaning of bodies that had slept on surfaces too hard for too long. Tam sat up, her dark hair a tangle, her face older than twelve in ways that had nothing to do with years.

"You're up early", she said. Her voice was rough with sleep.

Mira didn't answer. She was watching the door, the way the grey light fell across its surface, the way the handle stayed still when she willed it to move.

"She's not coming", Tam said. It wasn't a question.

"I don't know."

"They never come. The ones who leave. They don't come back."

Mira held Drift tighter. "She's different."

Tam laughed, a small sound without much humor in it. "They're all different. That's what they tell themselves. That's what the ones who left told themselves, right up until they didn't come back."

The twins woke then, gasping in unison, their eyes snapping open to stare at a ceiling that held nothing but shadow and wood. For a moment they were perfectly synchronized, two children breathing the same breath, and then the moment passed and they were separate again, blinking and confused and reaching for each other with hands that shook.

"The market", one of them said—the one with the scar above her eyebrow, the one Mira privately thought of as the leader. "Greysalt says we go today. All of us."

"Why all of us?" Tam asked.

The twin shrugged. "He didn't say. Just said we go. Said it's important."

Mira felt something cold move through her chest, a sensation she didn't have words for yet. It felt like the moment before the wave breaks, the held breath before the fall.

*Important.*

Greysalt used that word sometimes. *Important* that the crates be carried. *Important* that the floors be swept. *Important* that certain questions not be asked.

She'd never heard it attached to her before.

She didn't tell the others. She dressed in the clothes they'd given her—trousers too long, shirt too wide, shoes that flapped at the heel—and she splashed water on her face from the basin and she didn't look in the corroded mirror because she didn't want to see what her face was doing.

The breakfast was thin soup and bread that had been stale yesterday and was now something closer toite. She ate mechanically, tasting nothing, thinking about the market and the grey light and the way Greysalt had smiled when he'd told her about the surprise.

*They know about.*

She didn't know what Greysalt had meant. She'd heard him on the tideglass last night, speaking to someone whose voice came through thin and distorted, full of the static that living wires accumulated in a city built on salt and spite. She'd heard him say *they know about*, and then a long silence, and then something that might have been *bring her* or might have been *begin* or might have been nothing at all.

*They know about.*

They knew about what?

She finished her soup and set the bowl in the washing pile and she followed the others through the narrow corridors of the Wrecker's Promise, past the rooms where other children slept and ate and dreamed of families that might still want them, past the door that led to the upper deck where the morning air hit cold and sharp and tasted of salt and something else, something metallic, something she remembered from before.

The Gutter Market was a twenty-minute walk through the Flotilla's winding passages, a city within a city, a place where broken things found new purposes and new prices. Mira had been working there for three years, since she was old enough to carry a crate without dropping it. She knew the stalls and their owners, the good hiding spots, the exits that led to other exits. She knew which vendors would give her scraps if she looked hungry enough and which ones would call Greysalt if she lingered too long.

She knew the market like she knew her own heartbeat: constant, reliable, a rhythm she could count on.

Today it felt different.

The stalls were setting up, the vendors arranging their wares in patterns designed to catch the eye. The air smelled of fish and smoke and the particular sweetness of fruit that was almost too ripe, and underneath that something else, something that made Mira's skin prickle with awareness.

She couldn't name it. She didn't try.

Greysalt walked ahead, his bulk moving through the crowd like a ship through calm water, parting the flow of bodies without seeming to notice. He was wearing his good coat today, the one with the brass buttons, the one that made him look important. Mira had never thought about what *important* meant before. She was thinking about it now.

"You", he said, turning back to her. His smile was the same smile it had always been, but she was seeing it differently now, seeing the edges that hadn't been visible before. "You're with me."

The others peeled off toward their usual stations—the twins to the fish stalls, Tam to the textile area where she swept and sorted and tried not to be noticed by the merchants who sometimes looked at her like she was something they might want to purchase. Mira watched them go with a tightness in her chest that felt like预见like knowing.

Greysalt led her deeper into the market, past the familiar stalls into the areas where the vendors sold things that didn't have names, where the transactions happened in whispers and the currency was sometimes information and sometimes something less tangible. The light here was different—filtered through awnings and tarps and the accumulated weight of a city that had learned to hide its valuables.

"Wait here", Greysalt said. He pointed to a spot near a stall selling tideglass trinkets, watches and compasses and mirrors that showed reflections slightly out of sync with reality. "Someone's coming to talk to you."

"Who?"

Greysalt's smile didn't waver. "A friend. An old friend. You'll see."

He walked away, his bulk disappearing into the crowd, and Mira stood with Drift clutched against her chest and felt the cold spread from her fingers into her arms, into her chest, into the place where she kept the words she couldn't say.

*You could just stay.*

A woman was approaching. She was tall and thin, dressed in the dark clothes of someone who didn't want to be remembered, and she moved with a particular grace that Mira recognized without knowing why. Her face was angular, her hair grey and cropped short, her eyes the color of river water—grey and green and something else, something that caught the light and held it.

The woman stopped a few feet away. She didn't smile.

"Mira", she said. Her voice was rough, unused to saying the name.

Mira's throat closed around something that might have been a word or might have been nothing at all. She held Drift tighter. The stuffed bird's seams creaked in protest.

"You're supposed to be surprised", the woman said. "Greysalt said you were practicing."

"How did you—"

"Greysalt tells me things. He tells me a lot of things." The woman's eyes moved, cataloging exits, noting the crowd, the stalls, the shadows between them. She was doing it automatically, the way people breathe, and Mira recognized that motion from somewhere deep in her memory, from the before, from the time when she'd been held steady and the world had rocked beneath her. "He tells me you're working in the market now. He tells me you're healthy. He tells me you're happy."

"Am I?" Mira asked. It came out smaller than she intended.

The woman's face did something complicated, something that involved muscles fighting each other for control. "I don't know", she said. "I was hoping you could tell me."

The market moved around them, vendors calling their wares, customers bargaining for prices, children running between the stalls with the particular freedom of the young and unobserved. Mira was aware of none of it. She was aware only of the woman in front of her, the mother who had left and kept leaving and now stood close enough to touch and felt farther away than ever.

"I practiced", Mira said. "What I was going to say."

"What were you going to say?"

Mira looked down at Drift. The bird's button eyes stared back at her, impassive and patient. "I was going to say you could just stay. I was going to say you could stay and then I wouldn't have to miss you." She swallowed. "But you're not staying. You're going to leave again. That's why you're here. That's why Greysalt brought you."

The woman—her mother, her mother, her mother—went very still. It was the stillness of someone who had heard something they didn't expect, something that cut through defenses built over years of careful distance.

"Who told you that?"

"Nobody. I just know." Mira's voice was steady now, steadier than she'd expected. "I know things. Greysalt thinks I don't, but I do. I know about the foster arrangement. I know about the twins. I know about the bloodforge."

The word hung in the air between them, small and sharp and heavy with meaning.

The woman—Vera, her name was Vera, Mira had always known it even if she'd never said it—reached out. Her hand stopped just short of Mira's shoulder, trembling slightly, the hand of someone who had learned that touch was dangerous and had never quite managed to believe it anyway.

"Where did you hear that word?"

"The twins. They say it in their sleep. They say a lot of things." Mira looked up, meeting those river-water eyes. "I know you're Covenant. I know what that means. I know why I'm here."

"Do you?" Vera's voice was barely a whisper.

"No. But I know I'm not supposed to be."

Something shifted in Vera's face. The stillness remained, but it became a different kind of stillness—the stillness of decision, of calculation, of a mind that had never quite stopped being a weapon finally turning that weapon toward a new target.

"We need to leave", she said. "Now. Not later. Now."

"Greysalt—"

"Greysalt works for them. He always has." Vera's hand finally landed on Mira's shoulder, and the touch was electric, was warmth, was everything Mira had imagined and nothing she'd expected. "The message he sent me. *They know about.* He meant they know about you. They've always known. They used him to watch you, to make sure you were there when they needed you."

Mira's legs felt weak. The market seemed to tilt around her, familiar stalls becoming unfamiliar, the faces of the crowd resolving into shapes that suddenly seemed predatory.

"They need me for something."

"Yes."

"Something involving you."

"Yes."

Mira held Drift against her chest and felt the cold spreading through her again, the held breath before the fall, the moment before the wave breaks. She had known, somehow. She had always known. The arrangement was never about keeping her safe.

It was about keeping her until they were ready to use her.

"Where do we go?" she asked.

Vera's hand tightened on her shoulder. "Out. Through the market. There are people waiting—"

The voice came from behind them, smooth and cultured and carrying the particular authority of someone who had never been told no.

"Salt-Hand. I was hoping you'd make this easy."

Vera turned. Mira turned with her. A man was standing a few feet away, tall and lean, dressed in clothes that cost more than everything in the market combined. His face was handsome in a way that suggested careful maintenance, and his eyes were the color of old blood, dark and deep and knowing.

He smiled.

"Kael Morreth", Vera said. The name fell like a stone into water.

"The same. And you must be the daughter." Kael's smile widened. "Greysalt was right. She does have your eyes. It's remarkable, really, how the bloodforge breeds true."

Mira felt her mother's hand move to something at her waist, a motion so quick it was almost invisible. The air changed, charged with something that made Mira's skin prickle and her breath catch.

"You have no right", Vera said. Her voice was flat, controlled, the voice of someone who had killed thirty-seven people and was calculating whether this man should be thirty-eight.

"I have every right. The Covenant owns your contract. Your contract includes her." Kael gestured at Mira without looking at her, a dismissal so complete it felt like violence. "The bloodforge took something from her when she was born. Something that belongs to us now. We're simply collecting."

The cold in Mira's chest spread outward, into her limbs, into her fingers and toes. She thought of the woman who had given her Drift. *The taking. They always call it something prettier than that.*

"What did they take?" she asked. Her voice came out steady, older than seven, worn smooth by years of watching and waiting and learning not to ask questions she couldn't afford to have answered.

Kael finally looked at her. His eyes were curious now, clinical, the gaze of someone examining an interesting specimen.

"You'll understand soon", he said. "When you wake up in the Pale Tower and start to feel it. The hunger. The weight. The way everything you touch starts to—"

"Stop." Vera's hand emerged from her waistband holding something thin and bright and wrong in the grey morning light. "We're leaving. Now."

Kael didn't move. Behind him, Mira could see shapes in the crowd—men and women dressed in dark clothes, moving into position, cutting off exits she hadn't even known were exits.

"I don't think you are", Kael said. "I think you're going to come with us, both of you, and you're going to do exactly what we say, and you're going to be grateful that we're letting you keep each other at all." He paused. "The alternative is the correction houses. The place we send the ones who don't cooperate. I've seen what they do there. It's not pretty."

Mira felt her mother's stillness deepen, felt the weapon she carried lean toward something dangerous and irrevocable. She reached up and gripped Vera's free hand, small fingers wrapping around calloused palm, and she felt the tremor that ran through that grip, the war between violence and love.

*Mira*, her mother was thinking, she could feel it, *I'm sorry, I'm so sorry, I should have come for you years ago, I should have—*

"It's all right", Mira said. She didn't know if it was true. She said it anyway. "We'll go."

Vera's breath caught. Something shattered in her face, small and private, a hope she'd been holding breaking against the reality of the moment.

"Mira—"

"We'll go. And then we'll figure it out." Mira held Drift against her chest with one arm and her mother's hand with the other. "That's what we do, isn't it? We figure it out."

The market moved around them, oblivious, the ordinary chaos of commerce and survival continuing as if nothing had changed. Somewhere, the twins were sorting fish. Somewhere, Tam was sweeping floors. Somewhere, Greysalt was counting coins that didn't quite add up to what they should.

And here, in the grey morning light, a woman who had been trying to retire and a girl who had been trying to wait looked at each other and saw, for the first time, the shape of what they might become.

Together.

Kael smiled his careful smile. "Shall we?"

The tide was rising.

The water ran black and cold and deep, and in its depths the drowned places of the world kept their secrets, and somewhere in the Pale Tower something was waking up hungry, something that had been waiting for seven years to be fed.

Mira didn't look back.

She was learning that some things, once taken, could never be returned.
